item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the original source, which cannot be accessed, has no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey there,

Those are some fair critiques. This is my first (and currently only) submission as a skip, so I am hoping to write some more that have more substance.

That's actually a really interesting point. You're probably right, I can imagine that a seasoned researcher wouldn't have reacted that way in that situation. I don't think I'll change that part, because to be honest it took me a lot of work to even get it to that point, but that will be important to remember if/when I write more in the future!

Cheers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As it is, it's taking a lot of space to say that they're glasses that show the past, but the past they show is different. There's not really enough here to deduce anymore than that, and the last experiment log does more to muddle what exactly they're doing than really raise any creep or mystery. Overall, it's a nice idea, relatively well presented, but the article feels directionless. Also I feel like the researcher in the last experiment log freaks out a bit too easy. It feels more like the reaction of a somewhat skittish ordinary person than a person whose job it is to investigate anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Original author here,

Thank you so much! I appreciate your positivity regarding the article and my involvement. I just wanted to add something people enjoyed and could be appreciated, even if it's a relatively simple concept.

Cheers!

P.S. Are you saying a different SCP links to this one??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah my first article that I completely threw out had about that many paragraphs but was still shit lol. Sometimes less is more, and conciseness can be really powerful with SCP articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One can never thank enough the random mentions of older SCPs which one encounters when reading this or that. This dates back to before I joined the site, and I never saw it.

Excellent skip. +1. Too bad just one upvote allowed!

I also want to mention that it's very nice to read comments for a skip where the author steps in, asks, discusses, etc. Rare enough to be mentioned!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh I don't understand how this guy has 2 paragraphs of description while I do like 5 and I've never written a mainsite piece :P

But it is a cool idea, I definitely like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the minute I read the end of the description, this is a pretty rad concept. Seeing into the past, but with odd changes. I generally endorse this thing, but there were a few grammar mistakes that I can't look past, I'll just list 'em here.

  • "A junior Researcher…" For some reason, junior isn't capitalized, but researcher is, either you cap 'em both or have it as a common noun.
  • "first name spelled as Hannah…" It's 'spelt'. (Whoops, this is a UK exclusive Traditional English mistake.)

That's it, really, in regards to grammar.

My absolute favourite part of this are the testing logs, they're really well done, and insanely intriguing, just the nature of the SCP makes you think "What gonna happen?", the only thing you added to the concept was that there were slight changes and that seemed to add such an enormous layer of depth to the whole thing.

This was effin' cool, at the end bit, leaves that piece of thought for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, original author here.

Firstly, thank you for taking time to respond to this skip; I check periodically for new comments and to check the rating.

Uh, why would it break out of containment just because you looked at it through glasses? Unless you mean that it didn't break containment in the vision that Dr. A saw in the glasses? But wasn't his already established in the previous sentence?

Writing these addenda (and some of the description obviously) was very very tricky. I attempted to be as concise and non-confusing as possible, which, despite heavy revision, remained very difficult. I would gladly welcome help on making the addenda less confusing for first-time readers. The main issue I ran into was when I tried to really clearly make what was happening obvious, it seemed pedantic and/or was way too long and took away from the short/sweetness of the skip.

Now that I established that, to explain:
The idea is that SCP-████ did NOT break out of containment when seen through the glasses, even though the notes that the doctor took on the paper (as seen through the glasses) would have resulted in SCP-████ in breaking containment. To reiterate in a different way: Dr. A████████ noticed differences (while wearing the glasses) that SHOULD have lead to a security breach. In "real life" no security breach occurred and the view through the sunglasses did not lead to a security breach. This difference is important for showing that SCP-2993 is showing some inaccuracies, but that these inaccuracies seem… odd. I will refrain from sharing my own opinion of what SCP-2993 actually is because I feel like there are a number of ways to interpret SCP-2993.

Well, uh, yeah? Because they're differences from the real world, so by definition the real-world cage didn't include these differences? Unless you mean that the measurements were done on the cage in the 2993 world? What?

Dr. A████████ was not wearing the glasses while making measurements. This is to make sure the reader knows that the glasses are showing a different reality or lying or what have you. I am not sure how to correctly word a less confusing version of this. Do you have any suggestions?

Once again I appreciate your response. Cheers,

Ottuz

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entire Addendum SCP-2993-2 is just confusing and it's unclear what is it trying to tell us.

Approximately three hours later, when Dr. A████████ used SCP-2993 to observe the containment area of SCP-████, SCP-████ did not break free of containment

Uh, why would it break out of containment just because you looked at it through glasses? Unless you mean that it didn't break containment in the vision that Dr. A saw in the glasses? But wasn't this already established in the previous sentence?

and the differences in measurement observed by Dr. A████████ while wearing SCP-2993 were non-existent when he performed measurements of the containment himself.

Well, uh, yeah? Because they're differences from the real world, so by definition the real-world cage didn't include these differences? Unless you mean that the measurements were done on the cage in the 2993 world? What?

item.title: Re: A month later...
item.content:encoded: Thank you Zyn!

item.title: Re: A month later...
item.content:encoded: Ottuz, so you know, you can announce article updates here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1081677/update-thread-4 :)

item.title: A month later...
item.content:encoded: It's now been about a month since I first posted this SCP, and it's sitting at +5. I have substantially edited it in the past month to better fit a good article. I am just posting this to say that as far as I can tell at least, I'm done with all editing of the page. This is the final product.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This swung from uninteresting, to interesting, to stupid, back to interesting, before finally settling on bad dialogue. The description sets up a nice premise, and I was interested in whatever backstory you were building until I got to "the difference in length would normally lead to an XK-Class End of The World Scenario." I liked the destruction log, but the interview was just awful. It doesn't impart any new information and is very badly written. This has potential though. Just follow up on more of the implications instead of destroying it or being goofy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The destruction attempts log is excessive, but I like the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. A: HOLY [EXPLETIVE].

Stuff like this is silly. You don't need to censor expletives. This is a nitpick but it's really bothering to read something and then be totally taken out of it by censored swearing. Also don't know why you expunged the subject name of the person the interview is being done with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title's fine, and doesn't matter much. If anything, the simplicity of the title versus the object's effects are all the better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I vote for Living in the Past

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
This is both vague and incorrect advice. The simple act of adding experiments does nothing to improve the article if those experiments don't bring something to the table in terms of content.

If you're going to tell the author this, you need to explain what you think these experiments should reveal about the scip in question that the article hasn't yet. Otherwise they'll just be adding more dead weight.

Please defer to more experienced authors in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but the article seems incomplete.

You really need more experiments and time shenanigans to get the upvotes, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… a bit of a touch up here and there is needed, but mostly, I find it pretty good.

I'm gonna upvote for now, but I would be WAY more enthusiastic about it with some work.

If I could make two suggestions…

  • Maybe, make some more of those pictures. I'm sure that with a pair of sunglasses, you could make more pictures like the first one. Honestly, I would find it much more believable.
  • change the nickname. "A pair of sunglasses" just doesn't seem all that interesting. Maybe instead, "Living in the Past," or "See Life back then," or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people don't like expungments, just because it usually feels like the author couldn't imagine any more details, and just expunged some parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ottuz, you use [DATA EXPUNGED]/blackouts almost excessively. It makes the SCP very difficult to read comprehensively. Try to add more info and remove some of the classified bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to disagree with you here. Crosstesting for the sake of crosstesting, especially crosstests that don't make any sense, are definitely highly discouraged. But crosstests that make sense (and I do think that one w/ SCP-187 would make sense) are definitely allowable.

That said, there is a sizeable contingent of the audience that would downvote just because a crosstest is included, no matter how reasonable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That picture is excellent, just need to tweak the content some more :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The object has promise, but here's what I'd improve:

+ You'll have 20/20 vision...
- (puts on shades) ...in hindsight.

SCP-2993 appears to be an unremarkable standard pair of aviator sunglasses with no visible markings or logos and standard durability.

No need to say "unremarkable standard". Remove.

When viewed from the front, SCP-2993 is opaque and acts as a mirror…. No form of radiation is able to pass through SCP-2993. The fact that this breaks the laws of thermodynamics is pending investigation.

That doesn't actually break the laws of thermodynamics. Radiation can still be absorbed and reflected just fine. It's unexpected, but not thermo-breaking. Furthermore, given that the view from the other side isn't what's being shown in front of you, then no, light isn't getting through. Remove the thermo-breaking sentence.

SCP-2993 was recovered on ██/██/2005 in the home of Mr. ████████ ████, a ██████ Professor at [DATA EXPUNGED]. Foundation personnel were dispatched when Professor ██████ triggered multiple keywords on online forums under the pen name [DATA EXPUNGED].

Too much expunging. So a professor said something somewhere as someone. This is too non-informative. Furthermore, he's Professor ██████ elsewhere; shouldn't he be introduced as Professor ██████ and not Mr. ████████ ████? It's clearly not the same name, by the way. All in all, the Recovery section doesn't really give us enough. The guy bought it at a yard sale. Does the fact he's a professor matter? Or that he teaches something at something U? None of that needs expunging. Or recording.

Similarly, no need to mention names and then expunge them. "A Junior Researcher" can be referred to as "the researcher" later in the paragraph without multiple expungements.

APPROVED - O5-█

Put the number there. It's okay. The O5 is still unknowable.

The notes on SCP-████ appeared to be minimally different, excluding [DATA EXPUNGED].

This is one sentence where I don't mind the censorship. It's not relevant to SCP-2993, and is in keeping with the SCP theme.

What I love is that it seems that first, the glasses are showing the past. Then, the glasses are showing a parallel universe. Then, the glasses are just lying. Each theory requires different responses, and the ambiguity makes them all the more interesting.

No vote for now. It's not ready as is, but it has lots of potential with some revision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't have an issue with O5 mentioning in a Safe/Euclid/Keter/whatever if there was an actual point to the O5 having interest.

Yeah, you're misinterpreting me, but that's because I didn't make it clear myself. By 'trivial', I meant objects like these, things that no one of much importance really cares about because they pose no threat at all. Didn't mean to imply that all Safe/Euclid SCPs were trivial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is all just for the last bullet, and some of it is just clarification. The rest I pretty much agree with as is.

A) O5s personally watching/handling/whatever SCPs in general is frowned upon, although there are exceptions (thaumiel and a lot of the 001 proposals).

B) O5s and safes/euclids would be subject to the same criteria as O5s and keters.

C) (I may be misinterpreting you on this one) Safe and Euclid SCPs are not trivial. There are numerous safe and euclid scips that could exterminate the human race (SCP-008 being one of them). Object class has little to nothing to do with danger and is based almost entirely on difficulty of containment (the basic three, at least, thaumiel and other special ones have their own conditions).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Almost there, and I'd ordinarily novote, but there are multiple issues I have with this.

+ Feedback!
- Feedback!

First off, wording and tone. When you say stuff like:

SCP-2993 appears to be an unremarkable standard pair of aviator sunglasses with no visible markings or logos and standard durability.

Unremarkable is fine depending on how you use it; it's not good here, as it doesn't read nicely with the rest of the sentence and is padding. "Appears" is also something I have issue with. You wouldn't use it in this kind of context. If something is one thing but actually is another, then you'd use appears.

No form of radiation is able to pass through SCP-2993.

No form of radiation? So not even visible light?

The fact that this breaks the laws of thermodynamics is pending investigation.

This is silly. You don't need this sentence at all. You can let the reader figure that out on their own and imply it like you're doing. No need to state it. This also disrupts the flow of the rest of the description.

6 hours 17 minutes and 32 seconds

You repeat this often to the point where the article gets long to read and is tiring to see over and over. I'm not exactly sure if there's a better way to word it, but wouldn't it be better to simplify and make it "six hours"?

Secondly, your redaction and expungement. It's absolutely sloppy and drags the article down considerably. You don't need to expunge to the length you do.

SCP-2993 was recovered on ██/██/2005 in the home of Mr. ████████ ████, a ██████ Professor at [DATA EXPUNGED].

For example, this is really unneeded. "SCP-2993 was recovered on ██/██/2005 in the home of a professor at ████████ University." With that, you fixed it. The key thing here is that blackboxing dates is fine (even if people usually blackbox the year versus the month/day, but that's more of a tiny nitpick), but blackboxing the name of a person we hardly know or will care about is really not needed, and I have no clue what "██████ Professor" is supposed to be. And why expunge the location? Why not blackbox that out either?

You also repeatedly blackbox researcher and doctor names. Again, this is alright depending on the situation, but when all you can really see are blackboxes, you need to find a way to work around this. Either tell us who these people are, or limit the people you're mentioning. I recommend looking over the Zen and the Art of DATA EXPUNGED guide to get a better idea of how to work at it.

Third, you can easily cut down on the text of a lot of this. The recovery section in particular uses a lot of words to say nothing interesting or of importance, and you do the same thing in the addenda too. All the doctors' notes don't really need to be there, especially not this:

-I am now questioning whether SCP-2993 is viewing into another universe as had been first theorized, or if, instead of showing a parallel universe, SCP-2993 shows the past of our universe in an imperfect manner with some minor differences. - Dr. ████████

Again, this is something you would imply and let the reader try to figure out on their own. Don't spoonfeed it to them.

Finally, there's not much of a story here. True, you leave implications (even if you outright tell the reader what to assume at times) and they're interesting, but it doesn't really lead me to care much beyond that. Give the readers more, give them breadcrumbs and a story, even if it's subtle, to let them know the larger implications of this. Because otherwise, these are just sunglasses that let the user see into a parallel universe.

- Feedback!

I like this idea. I think it can be done well, and there's potential for it to be interesting. But there's too much here that isn't done well that makes me downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, but with SCPs, it's less "east wing of the building" and more "eastern hemisphere, the solar system, the galaxy, the space-time continuum, etc."

Basic idea: if you don't know how things will react, you will be extremely careful with regards to exposing them to each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding Scorpion here.
I was just about to make the same point.

It's not that cross testing is forbidden, but it:

…isn't something that's done on a "just to see what would happen" basis these days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Few thoughts.

  • No reason to say it violates laws of thermodynamics - we know what they are and it is anomalous.
  • The recovery log adds nothing to the article. It just says the glasses might be a failed experiment - but nothing truly gripping.
  • I'm finding it very hard to believe that a doctor JUST so happened to find out information on how to stop an XK right in the nick of time. We already know the glasses do what they do - making them inadvertently save the world is unnecessarily dramatic.
  • I'm starting to get sick of all this O5 referencing in relatively normal Euclid and Safe SCPs. Why are the leaders of the Foundation worrying themselves over these trivial things? Shouldn't this be site staff? Sorry if my headcanon is leaking but it just seems so pointless to throw in O5 notes.

All in all, I'm not sure. Granted, the picture is neat and the writing is alright concerning tonal errors. But nothing here really was expanded upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, you seem eager for advice, so I'm gonna go over some of the reasons I downvoted. I read the containment procedures, and they're pretty good. Brief, basic, clear. So nice job. The skip then however, didn't have the same pleasure. The effect itself is mildly interesting. The type of thing that if given it's own twist could work well as an SCP, but otherwise could not. Personally, I don't really see any twist that persuaded me towards maybe upvoting.

+ show block
– hide block

The recovery log doesn't feel necessary. Your redactions here seem fine to me, but I'm not particularly interested in what happened because there's no real narrative or conflict.

The addendum-1 could be potentially interesting as an introduction into a more expansive anomaly, but it seems bogged down in the story with the breach, and this:

It should be noted that both sentences Junior Researcher B█████ wrote contain all twenty-six letters of the alphabet.

Eh, I don't care all that much. It feels random and out of place. Why should this be noted? Overall, I feel like the second paragraph of addendum one was the only thing with any real value. I also disapprove of an O5 being needed to approve testing with such a basic object. It also makes me question why they were allowed to view video footage when they weren't allowed to test.

Addendum-2 is where expungement gets a bit out of hand for me. Potentially interesting information like what changed, and what could lead to and XK event, are simply expunged. The doctor asks permission again for some reason, even though he already has permission, and then you go into trying to destroy the object. This is something I have a big issue with. What if the object does get destroyed? Considering it's supposed to look into another universe, one with XK class scenarios in place, what happens if you break connection with this universe? The fact is, we don't know, if anything something terrible. I would never permit these tests.

Finally, I think the theorizations (both of them) about the parallel universes are a slight break in clinical tone. Not only that, but even without them the reader can easily make similar conclusions without it being said outright. The second time I saw the doctor's quote concerning this idea, it felt really redundant.


Anyways, my opinions. I would have no-voted if only one or two of these were present, but since I can identify things that I dislike in almost all of the portions in the skip I decided to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd just like to point out that crosstesting isn't something that's done on a "just to see what would happen" basis these days. Interactions between two anomalous things, neither of which you understand completely, can be unpredictable.

Basically, trying learn something about two SCP's by crosstesting them is on par with trying to learn something about two unlabeled reagents you found in the supply room of a chemistry lab by dumping them together in a beaker. Best case scenario they react in some radical way that tells you something, worst case scenario you just blew yourself up and took the east wing of the building with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture was 100% the reason for the post, so I guess that makes sense. I don't blame you. Anything you can suggest for me to improve?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would you mind elaborating more for me??

I'm interested in improving my writing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same. I'm not a big fan of censorship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt a need to upvote, but realized it was mostly due to the nifty-cool picture and nothing else. Not bad, but not gonna stick with me otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't have any strong feelings for or against it, but the poor use of [DATA EXPUNGED]' really ruined it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Howdy!

First off, thank you for the positive response, I appreciate it! As this is my first SCP I have written, I did not at all want to do one of the "no-no's" listed and mention another SCP to gain recognition for this SCP / all that jazz. If there's enough support I would be more than willing to contact the original/current author of SCP-187 and talk to her/him about their opinion on the subject and see if we can't collaborate something.

I really appreciate the support. If I feel like I can continue to get support for this SCP, I would totally be down for writing more. The only issue is that it is such a time commitment, and I probably edited this over 20 times in a 6 month period (so I could re-read it fresh each time) in order to make sure it was good enough to go to the page! Also, big thanks to "themanofum" (I don't know if there's a way to tag users?) for all the support and help he gave me to bring this SCP to it's end-game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, more time shenanigans!

So these glasses let the wearer see into the "past" to an oddly specific amount of time and The Foundation is thinking they may not be looking at the past, but parallel universes that are just running slow in comparison to ours.

I like this one and only have one suggestion: Try giving them to…ugh, why can I never remember names, that one SCP that can see the future as she views the present. I know it mentions her in the guide to writing humanoid SCP's, but I can't remember her number.

I'd like to know what would happen if the SCP that can see the future at the same time as the present wore glasses that made her see the past. How much reality would explode because of this? :)

Edit: Found it. SCP-187 is the woman that sees the future and past as one.
